content.text: A DPIA is not simply a risk register. Article 35(7) GDPR prescribes the minimum content: a description of the processing and its purposes; an assessment of the necessity and proportionality of the processing relative to those purposes; an assessment of the risks to data subjects; and the measures envisaged to address those risks. Each section requires substantive analysis, not boilerplate.

For Claude deployments, the DPIA must also address the fact that the processing is mediated by a third-party AI provider (Anthropic), which introduces a supply-chain element that most standard DPIA templates do not cover well. The template structure below accommodates this.

A DPIA that consists entirely of tick-boxes and generic controls is unlikely to withstand regulatory scrutiny. The EDPB has stated that DPIAs must demonstrate genuine engagement with the specific risks of the processing in question. For novel AI processing, 'we use encryption and access controls' is not a sufficient risk mitigation plan.

attrs.alt: 3x3 risk matrix showing likelihood (High/Medium/Low) vs severity (Low/Medium/High) with example Claude processing risks mapped to cells: Very High through Negligible

content.text: Section-by-Section DPIA Template for Claude Features

1. PROCESSING DESCRIPTION

1.1 Feature name and owner
    Feature: [Name]
    Business owner: [Name, department]
    Technical owner: [Name, team]
    DPO: [Name]
    Date: YYYY-MM-DD | Review date: YYYY-MM-DD

1.2 Purpose and legal basis
    Primary purpose: [What the feature does and why]
    Legal basis (Article 6 GDPR):
      ☐ Consent (Article 6(1)(a)) — note: freely given? specific? informed? withdrawable?
      ☐ Contract (Article 6(1)(b))
      ☐ Legal obligation (Article 6(1)(c))
      ☐ Vital interests (Article 6(1)(d))
      ☐ Public task (Article 6(1)(e))
      ☑ Legitimate interests (Article 6(1)(f)) — LIA required [attach]
    Additional basis for special category data (Article 9):
      [If applicable: explicit consent / employment law / substantial public interest / etc.]

1.3 Data inventory
    ┌─────────────────────┬──────────────┬────────────────┬──────────────────┐
    │ Data category       │ Source       │ Subjects       │ Volume estimate  │
    ├─────────────────────┼──────────────┼────────────────┼──────────────────┤
    │ [e.g. Name, email]  │ [CRM system] │ [Customers]    │ [~50k/month]     │
    │ [e.g. Health data]  │ [EHR system] │ [Patients]     │ [~1k/day]        │
    └─────────────────────┴──────────────┴────────────────┴──────────────────┘

1.4 Data flows
    [Describe how data moves: user → your application → Claude API → response → storage]
    [Include: what enters the prompt, what is returned, what is logged, where it is stored]
    [Identify all processors and sub-processors, including Anthropic]

1.5 Retention
    Conversation logs: [period / policy]
    Model outputs: [period / policy]
    Anthropic API retention: [confirm per Anthropic DPA — typically zero retention without opt-in]

2. NECESSITY AND PROPORTIONALITY

2.1 Necessity assessment
    Could the processing purpose be achieved with less personal data?
    [Specific analysis — not generic. E.g.: "We require the employee's name and department
    to personalise responses; their salary and performance rating are not required and are
    excluded from the context window. We have confirmed this by reviewing the system prompt
    and the data fields passed from the HRIS integration."]

2.2 Data minimisation measures implemented
    ☐ Only required fields passed to Claude context window (no full records)
    ☐ Anonymisation / pseudonymisation applied before processing where possible
    ☐ Session data deleted at conversation end (not logged)
    ☐ System prompt contains no personal data
    [List specific technical controls implemented]

2.3 Proportionality
    Is the privacy intrusion justified by the purpose?
    [Analysis: what benefit does the processing provide? To whom? Is that benefit
    proportionate to the risk and intrusion to data subjects?]

2.4 Data subject rights accommodation
    ☐ Right of access: how will subjects access their data?
    ☐ Right to rectification: how are errors corrected?
    ☐ Right to erasure: what triggers deletion? How is it executed?
    ☐ Right to object: can subjects opt out of AI processing specifically?
    ☐ Rights related to automated decision-making (Article 22): [applicable? safeguards?]

This is the substantive core of the DPIA. For each identified risk, the assessment must cover the nature of the harm to data subjects, its likelihood before mitigation, and its severity if it materialises.
Prompt injection causes system prompt exfiltration containing PII
Input validation; context isolation; no PII in system prompt
Indirect injection via retrieved documents alters Claude's tool calls
RAG document sanitisation; tool-call confirmation for destructive actions
Conversation logs retained longer than necessary, creating breach surface
Automated log deletion at 30 days; access controls on log store
Claude infers sensitive attributes (health, political) from conversational patterns
No inference-based profiling; output filtering for sensitive inferences
Cross-border data transfer to Anthropic's US infrastructure without adequate safeguards
Anthropic DPA with SCCs; confirm adequacy mechanism in force
Automated employment decision made by Claude without mandatory human review
Mandatory human review gate before any employment decision is actioned
The risk mitigation plan translates the risk register into concrete engineering and governance actions with owners and deadlines.

4. RISK MITIGATION PLAN

For each control in the risk register, document:

Control ID: C-01
Risk addressed: R1 (prompt injection / system prompt exfiltration)
Control type: ☑ Technical  ☐ Organisational  ☐ Contractual
Description: Implement an input sanitisation layer that strips known prompt injection
patterns before user input reaches the Claude API call. Additionally, the system prompt
is reviewed to confirm it contains no personal data — if personalisation is required,
it is injected per-call from a parameterised template, not embedded in the static prompt.
Implementation owner: [Backend engineering team lead]
Target completion: YYYY-MM-DD
Validation method: Security test covering top-20 known prompt injection payloads;
penetration test of system prompt recovery via jailbreak attempts
Status: ☐ Not started  ☐ In progress  ☑ Complete  ☐ Verified

---

Control ID: C-05
Risk addressed: R5 (cross-border transfer)
Control type: ☐ Technical  ☐ Organisational  ☑ Contractual
Description: Execute Anthropic's Data Processing Agreement including Standard Contractual
Clauses (Module 2, Controller-to-Processor) before initiating any API calls. Confirm that
the EU-US Data Privacy Framework certification is current for Anthropic and update
the adequacy mechanism reference if Anthropic's certification status changes.
Implementation owner: [Legal / procurement]
Target completion: Before any API call with EU personal data
Validation method: DPA signed and filed; DPO confirms SCCs are current; annual review
Status: ☐ Not started  ☑ In progress  ☐ Complete  ☐ Verified
Generating a DPIA Risk Register with Claude

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def generate_dpia_risk_register(feature_description: str, data_inventory: list[dict]) -> dict:
    """
    Generate a structured DPIA risk register for a Claude feature.
    Returns identified risks with likelihood, severity, and suggested controls.
    """
    prompt = f"""You are a GDPR data protection expert specialising in AI systems.
Generate a DPIA risk register for the following Claude-powered feature.

Feature description:
{feature_description}

Data inventory (categories and subjects):
{json.dumps(data_inventory, indent=2)}

Identify 5-8 specific, non-generic risks. For each risk:
1. Describe the harm to data subjects concretely (not "risk of data breach" — instead
   "an attacker could inject a prompt that causes Claude to include the patient's
   diagnosis in a response visible to a different user")
2. Assess likelihood as "High" | "Medium" | "Low" before controls are applied
3. Assess severity as "Very High" | "High" | "Medium" | "Low"
4. Suggest 2-3 specific technical or organisational controls
5. Estimate residual risk after controls as "High" | "Medium" | "Low" | "Negligible"

Pay particular attention to:
- Prompt injection and indirect injection risks specific to this deployment
- Context window isolation risks (can one user's data leak to another?)
- AI-specific risks: unintended inferences, hallucinated personal facts, unauthorised
  tool-call execution
- Anthropic as a data processor: cross-border transfer, sub-processor risks

Respond as a JSON object with key "risks" containing a list of risk objects."""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=2048,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)


feature = """
A Claude-powered clinical documentation assistant for hospital nurses. The nurse
dictates or types notes about a patient visit; Claude restructures them into
a standardised SOAP note format and suggests ICD-10 codes. Notes include
patient name, DOB, diagnosis, medications, and treatment plan. The structured
output is inserted directly into the EHR system. Nurses can correct the output
before saving, but the default action is to save Claude's version without review.
"""

inventory = [
    {"category": "Health data (diagnosis, medications, treatment)", "subjects": "Patients", "volume": "~500/day", "sensitivity": "Article 9 special category"},
    {"category": "Name, date of birth, NHS number", "subjects": "Patients", "volume": "~500/day", "sensitivity": "Standard personal data"},
    {"category": "Nurse login and action logs", "subjects": "Employees", "volume": "~50/day", "sensitivity": "Standard personal data"},
]

result = generate_dpia_risk_register(feature, inventory)
for i, risk in enumerate(result["risks"], 1):
    print(f"Risk {i}: {risk.get('description', risk.get('risk_description', 'N/A'))}")
    print(f"  Likelihood: {risk.get('likelihood_pre_control')} | Severity: {risk.get('severity')}")
    print(f"  Residual: {risk.get('residual_risk')}")
    print()

When the DPIA concludes that residual risk remains high after all practical controls are applied, Article 36 GDPR requires the controller to consult the supervisory authority before beginning processing. This is not optional — it is a legal requirement with a statutory process:
Submit the completed DPIA to the supervisory authority (DPA) with a written request for prior consultation.
The DPA has 8 weeks to respond (extendable by 6 weeks for complex cases).
The DPA may provide written advice, impose conditions on the processing, or prohibit it.
Processing must not begin until the DPA's consultation period has elapsed and any conditions have been addressed.

if your DPIA for a Claude deployment identifies a residual Very High risk (e.g., automated employment decisions without human review in a large enterprise), you must allow for up to 14 weeks of regulatory consultation before the feature can launch. This should be factored into product roadmap planning.
What the DPA Looks for in an AI DPIA

Can you explain how Claude reached a specific output? For decisions affecting data subjects, supervisory authorities expect at least a high-level explanation of the factors Claude considered — not a neuron-level technical description, but a human-readable account.

Do data subjects have a meaningful way to challenge a Claude-generated decision? A nominal review process where the human reviewer has no practical ability to override Claude's output is not sufficient.
For high-risk decisions, the DPA expects evidence that human review is genuine and not ceremonial. This means the reviewer has time, training, and authority to change the outcome.
The DPIA should commit to periodic review of the feature's risk profile — at least annually, and whenever the processing or model changes materially.
Integrating the DPIA into Feature Documentation

A completed DPIA should not sit in a compliance folder, unread after sign-off. Its risk mitigation commitments become engineering requirements; its data inventory becomes the authoritative record for breach notification purposes; its retention schedule becomes a task for the data infrastructure team.
Data flow diagram; ROPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry
Engineering tickets in sprint backlog; Definition of Done checklist items
Executed DPA with Anthropic; sub-processor register entry
Support runbook for DSARs; deletion workflow implementation
Data lifecycle policy; automated deletion job
Calendar reminder; change management trigger condition

A DPIA for a Claude deployment consists of four substantive sections: a processing description (data inventory, flows, retention, legal basis), a necessity and proportionality assessment, a risk register (likelihood × severity for Claude-specific harms), and a risk mitigation plan with owned controls and validation methods. The risk matrix guides response: negligible and low risks are accepted or monitored; medium risks receive standard controls; high risks require mandatory mitigations; very high residual risks require prior consultation with the supervisory authority before deployment. DPIA risk registers can be generated with Claude itself to accelerate drafting, but the DPO must review and own the final assessment. The DPIA's commitments flow directly into engineering requirements and data infrastructure tasks — a DPIA that influences no implementation decisions has not served its purpose.
